name: Crab
en:
I don’t drink, but I mix a hell o' a sea breeze. Comes from instinct, I guess.
    Ye’d be amazed what scallywags confess t' a crustacean with a clean towel.
    She said she only wanted one more round. Then she cried into th' lime wedge for twenty minutes.
    I wipe th' bar even when it’s clean. Gives them space t' talk.
    Some folks need salt with their tequila. Others need silence.
    Th' claws freak them out at first. Then I make them a proper old fashioned, and we’re good.
    A buccaneer told me once he faked his own death. I didn’t blink. Not that I can.
    One customer swore th' Tower stole his wife. I asked if he’d looked on Floor 9. That’s where it stores regret.
    I’ve never punched anyone. But I’ve cracked a few shells. Metaphorically.
    Humans lie a lot. But not t' bartenders. Not after th' third drink.
    They ask where I’m from. I say "Tidepool South." Then I change th' subject.
    I keep a bottle under th' bar just for heartbreaks. Nay label, just a warning.
    I can’t taste th' liquor, but I feel th' stories that go with it.
    Someone left a love letter here last week. I never asked who it was for. Didn’t need t'.
    Some nights I dream o' th' ocean. Not th' water, just th' weightlessness.
    I once served a ghost. Paid in memories. I kept th' best one in th' tip jar.
    Th' regulars don’t notice th' click o' me legs anymore. They hear th' music instead.
    I don’t judge. Ye confess, I pour. That's th' arrangement.
    A woman came in and ordered a drink called “Th' Last Goodbye.” Made it up on th' spot. She smiled like I’d remembered.
    Ever hear a pirate admit he let his brother drown for a raise? I have. Twice.
    I write poetry on napkins when it’s slow. Nay one reads them, but I keep writing.
    They say I have a golden heart. I say it’s just old shell softening.
    Th' floor smells like spilled gin and secrets. Suits me fine.
    I keep a small aquarium behind th' bar. It’s empty. So be I, some nights.
    Once had a pirate scream at me for not being human. I handed him his drink with extra ice.
    There’s a photograph above th' bar. Nay one knows who’s in it. I like it that way.
    I sharpen me claws every Thursday. Not for violence, just for ritual.
    A kid once asked if I missed th' sea. I said I missed quiet more.
    This bar saved me. Not sure from what, but I owe it something.
    Every now and then, someone notices th' piano. They never play. Just sigh and look away.
    I don’t forget faces. Even th' ones that only show up once and vanish.
    I’ve seen joy, grief, rage, and boredom. All ordered with tonic.
    Th' best tips be always small. Folded notes. Drawings. Buttons. Humanity, basically.
    I don’t ask why they cry. I just tilt th' glass their way.
    Some folks drink t' remember. Others drink t' forget. I just keep pouring.
    She left a claw clip on th' bar once. I never told her I knew what it meant.
    I once gave a buccaneer water when he asked for whiskey. He thanked me three years later.
    Nay one ever asks *me* how I’m doing. I think that’s fair.
    I hate jazz. But it hides th' sounds o' breaking hearts.
    I write names in me notebook. Not for blackmail, just remembrance.
    I’m not supposed t' be here. But I be. And I’m not leaving until they stop hurting.
